# Customise the output file

`pip-compile` produces a deterministic file by default: a header, index options, then pinned packages
with `# via` annotations. Several flags control which parts get emitted and how.

## The header

The header is the comment block at the top of the file:

```text
#
# This file is autogenerated by pip-compile with Python 3.13
# by the following command:
#
#    pip-compile --output-file=requirements.txt pyproject.toml
#
```

`--no-header` strips it entirely:

```console
pip-compile --no-header -o requirements.txt pyproject.toml
```

Use this when you want a clean diff that does not include the command line, or when a wrapper script
manages the header itself.

To override the command line shown without removing the header, set `CUSTOM_COMPILE_COMMAND` in the
environment. See {doc}`custom-compile-command`.

## Annotations

Annotations are the `# via` comments under each pinned line:

```text
django==4.2.7
    # via my-app (pyproject.toml)
asgiref==3.7.2
    # via django
```

`--no-annotate` removes them:

```console
pip-compile --no-annotate
```

`--annotation-style` chooses between two layouts:

::::{tab} split (default)

```text
django==4.2.7
    # via
    #   -c constraints.txt
    #   django-debug-toolbar
asgiref==3.7.2
    # via django
```

Multi-line when several sources contributed; one line each when one did.
::::

::::{tab} line

```text
django==4.2.7              # via -c constraints.txt, django-debug-toolbar
asgiref==3.7.2             # via django
```

Flat at the cost of long lines. Pass `--annotation-style line`.
::::

## Index options

By default the file repeats the index URLs and trusted hosts that `pip-compile` saw, so a downstream
`pip install -r` uses the same indexes:

```text
--index-url https://pypi.org/simple
--extra-index-url https://my-private-index.example.com/simple
--trusted-host my-private-index.example.com
```

Several flags strip these:

- `--no-emit-index-url` removes `--index-url` and `--extra-index-url`.
- `--no-emit-find-links` removes `--find-links` lines.
- `--no-emit-trusted-host` removes `--trusted-host` lines.
- `--no-emit-options` removes all of the above plus format controls.

The common reason to strip: the lockfile is checked into a public repo and the URLs leak the existence of
a private mirror. Strip them in the file, set them in CI via environment variables instead.

## Line endings

`--newline` controls line endings in the output:

- `LF` writes `\n`.
- `CRLF` writes `\r\n`.
- `native` uses the platform's `os.linesep`.
- `preserve` (default) detects from the existing output file or input files; falls back to `\n`.

```console
pip-compile --newline=LF
```

`preserve` is right for cross-platform projects. If a Windows engineer commits a CRLF file and a Linux
engineer commits an LF re-compile, the second commit reverts the first's line endings. `preserve` looks
at what's already there and keeps it.

## Hash output

With `--generate-hashes`, each package gets multi-line hash entries:

```text
django==4.2.7 \
    --hash=sha256:abc... \
    --hash=sha256:def...
    # via my-app (pyproject.toml)
```

`--reuse-hashes` (on by default) copies hashes from the existing output file when the pin still
satisfies. `--no-reuse-hashes` forces a refresh. See {doc}`use-hashes`.

## Stripping extras

`--strip-extras` drops the `[bracketed]` part of requirement lines:

```text
requests==2.31.0    # without --strip-extras: requests[security]==2.31.0
```

Required if you want the file usable as a `PIP_CONSTRAINT` target. The default flips on in
`pip-tools 8.0.0`. See {doc}`/explanation/strip-extras-and-constraints`.

## Putting it all together

A "minimal-diff, audit-friendly" config:

```toml
[tool.pip-tools]
generate-hashes = true
strip-extras = true
allow-unsafe = true
no-emit-trusted-host = true
no-emit-find-links = true
annotation-style = "line"
```

Flags can also live on the command line if you want per-invocation control.
